title: Higher Dimensional Fractal Uncertainty
speaker: Alex Cohen
abstract: A fractal uncertainty principle (FUP) roughly says that a function and its Fourier transform cannot both be concentrated on a fractal set. These were introduced to harmonic analysis in order to prove new results in quantum chaos: if eigenfunctions on hyperbolic manifolds concentrated in unexpected ways, that would contradict the FUP. Bourgain and Dyatlov proved FUP over the real numbers, and in this talk I will discuss an extension to higher dimensions. The bulk of the

proof_stages.label: Quantum-chaos motivation for fractal uncertainty
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates fractal uncertainty as a Fourier-analytic input that yields lower mass bounds for high-frequency eigenfunctions on compact hyperbolic manifolds, beyond volume and entropy estimates.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional porosity and FUP
proof_stages.summary: Porous subsets of the line are defined across scales, and the Bourgain-Dyatlov one-dimensional fractal uncertainty principle is stated as a norm bound for functions and Fourier transforms localized on porous h-scale sets.

proof_stages.label: Line porosity and the high-dimensional theorem
proof_stages.summary: The naive higher-dimensional replacement by ball porosity is shown to fail, so the speaker introduces porosity on lines and states the main high-dimensional FUP with one set ball-porous and the dual set line-porous.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to quantitative unique continuation
proof_stages.summary: Following the Bourgain-Dyatlov strategy, the FUP is reduced to a quantitative unique continuation lower bound on thick sampling sets, and then to constructing nonzero damping functions whose Fourier transforms decay rapidly on the fractal frequency set.

proof_stages.label: Multiplier theorem and growth hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The Beurling-Malliavin multiplier theorem and its higher-dimensional analogue are presented as the mechanism for producing damping functions from weights satisfying regularity and growth conditions, with the higher-dimensional growth condition detecting mass along lines.

proof_stages.label: Paley-Wiener and plurisubharmonic reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Paley-Wiener converts compact Fourier support into entire-function growth, and taking logarithms turns the multiplier construction into a potential-theoretic obstacle problem for subharmonic or plurisubharmonic functions.

proof_stages.label: Pluripotential construction and Hörmander completion
proof_stages.summary: The required plurisubharmonic weight is constructed via a linewise harmonic extension operator plus a modified lower weight, and Hörmander L2 estimates for the del-bar equation convert this weight back into an entire function, hence a damping multiplier.

examples.name: Cantor set as a porous set
examples.note: The iterative removal construction of a Cantor set makes porosity and h-scale truncation concrete, but it only illustrates the definition and the one-dimensional theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the Cantor set is named as the basic porous example and its iterative interval-removal construction is briefly described, but no property is concretely checked on it

passes.reason: The Cantor set is used as a standard representative porous fractal, not as a special obstruction or boundary case.

examples.name: Dual thin rectangles defeating ball porosity
examples.note: A tall thin rectangle and its Fourier-dual wide skinny rectangle are porous on balls yet nearly saturate the Fourier transform, visibly forcing the move from ball porosity to line porosity.
examples.engagement_reason: the thin dual rectangles are analyzed as an obstruction: their Fourier duality is identified, ball porosity is checked, and failure of line porosity is tested by placing a line segment along the rectangle

passes.reason: The highly eccentric line-like rectangles at dual Fourier scales are boundary packets used to show ball-porosity alone does not give the higher-dimensional FUP.
The Fourier-dual skinny rectangles are slab-like limiting sets used to show ball porosity alone does not imply the higher-dimensional FUP.
The reciprocal skinny rectangles are line-like, uncertainty-tight boxes used to show ball porosity alone does not imply the higher-dimensional FUP.
The skinny dual rectangles are line-like reciprocal-scale tube cases that make ball porosity insufficient for the FUP.
The dual tall/thin rectangles are line-like limiting, extreme-aspect-ratio sets used explicitly to show ball porosity does not suffice in higher dimensions.
the reciprocal thin rectangles are essentially line-like uncertainty packets used to show ball porosity alone fails in higher dimensions.
The reciprocal thin rectangles form a tight Fourier-dual, near-lower-dimensional obstruction showing ball porosity is insufficient.
The reciprocal thin rectangles are line-like Fourier-dual boundary boxes used to show that ball porosity alone cannot give the higher-dimensional FUP.
the dual skinny rectangles are the obstruction showing ball-porosity alone fails in higher-dimensional FUP, and their usefulness comes from extreme anisotropic aspect ratios
The reciprocal thin rectangles are an almost line-like, uncertainty-saturating obstruction to ball-porosity FUP.
The dual thin rectangles are line-like anisotropic boundary cases saturating Fourier uncertainty and are introduced specifically to show ball-porosity is insufficient.
The reciprocal thin rectangles are near lower-dimensional anisotropic boundary cases used to show ball porosity alone fails.

examples.name: Logarithmic decay weights
examples.note: The trial weights with decay like -|x|, -|x|/log|x|, and -|x|/(log|x|)^2 illustrate the sharp growth threshold in the multiplier theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: specific logarithmic decay weights are compared against the growth condition, with the speaker deciding which decay rates are too strong and which are admissible

passes.reason: The radial logarithmic decay weights are rotation-invariant model weights sitting at the sharp borderline for allowable decay.

The radial logarithmic weights illustrate the sharp borderline between allowable and too-fast decay rather than serving as an adversarial counterexample.
The radial logarithmically corrected weights illustrate the critical growth/decay threshold for the multiplier theorem.

examples.name: Polynomials and zero measures
examples.note: For a polynomial, the Laplacian of log modulus is a sum of delta masses at the roots, and this example motivates replacing discrete zero data by flexible positive measures/subharmonic functions.
examples.engagement_reason: polynomials are used concretely by computing the Laplacian of the log magnitude as a sum of delta masses at roots and interpreting this as a measure

passes.reason: Polynomial zero data give finite atomic sums of Dirac masses, a collapsed measure model used only to motivate subharmonic potentials.
Polynomials are used as an ordinary model for analytic functions whose zero divisor gives a positive atomic measure, not as a failing case.
The polynomial zero measure is a finite atomic sum of delta masses at roots, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
Polynomials are a standard analytic model whose zeros give an atomic positive measure to motivate subharmonicity.
Polynomials and their zero-counting measures are used as an ordinary model for log-magnitude subharmonicity and positive measures.
polynomial zero-counting measures are finite sums of point masses, used illustratively as the atomic model for general positive measures.
Polynomials are used as a simple model whose zero divisor is a finite atomic measure concentrated at points.
